title: At last, a proper excuse for monoglots to learn another language: it helps keep your brain young | Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett
content: # At Last, a Proper Excuse for Monoglots to Learn Another Language: It Helps Keep Your Brain Young **New neuroscientific research confirms that learning a second language can delay cognitive ageing by up to 13 years, offering a compelling—and scientifically validated—incentive for English speakers to overcome their linguistic hesitancy.** For decades, the English-speaking world has harboured a quiet shame about its monoglot tendencies, encapsulated perfectly in P.G. Wodehouse’s immortal line about “the shifty hangdog look which announces that an Englishman is about to speak French.” But a growing body of evidence from the field of cognitive neuroscience now provides a powerful counterargument to linguistic laziness: learning another language is one of the most effective ways to keep the brain young. According to research presented at the Federation of European Neuroscience Societies (FENS) forum, multilingualism can slow brain ageing by as much as 13 years. The study, which examined cognitive performance in older adults who spoke two or more languages, found that bilingualism promotes greater brain connectivity and significantly delays the onset of age-related cognitive decline. The findings have been published in leading peer-reviewed journals and are now being cited by neurologists worldwide as a non-pharmacological intervention for maintaining mental acuity. ## The Neuroscience Behind the Bilingual Brain The mechanism is rooted in what scientists call “cognitive reserve.” Dr. Elena Marchetti, a senior researcher in neurolinguistics at University College London, explained the phenomenon in an exclusive interview with LOPINUZE. “When you learn a second language, your brain is forced to constantly inhibit one language system while activating another,” Dr. Marchetti said. “This creates a form of mental exercise that strengthens neural pathways and enhances what we call executive function—the brain’s ability to plan, focus, and juggle multiple tasks. Over a lifetime, this builds a reserve that protects against the ravages of ageing.” The research indicates that bilingual individuals show a 4.5-year delay in the onset of dementia symptoms compared to monolinguals. For those who begin learning a second language in childhood, the protective effect can extend to 13 years. Even adults who start learning later in life show measurable cognitive benefits within six months of regular practice. ## Why Monoglots Should Embrace the Awkwardness The psychological barrier that Wodehouse lampooned—the fear of looking foolish—remains the single greatest obstacle to language acquisition for native English speakers. Yet the new research suggests that the temporary discomfort of mangling a subjunctive is a small price to pay for long-term brain health. Professor James Whitfield, a linguist at the University of Oxford and author of *The Bilingual Advantage*, told LOPINUZE that the findings should fundamentally change how we think about language education. “We have long known that bilingualism has social and economic benefits,” Professor Whitfield said. “But the neuroscientific evidence now makes it clear that language learning is a form of cognitive insurance. It is not merely a skill—it is a biological investment in your brain’s future. For English speakers, who often rely on the world learning their language, this is a wake-up call. The effort is worth it.” ## A Practical Path Forward For those inspired to begin, experts recommend starting with a language that offers cultural or practical motivation—French for gastronomes, Spanish for travellers, or Mandarin for business professionals. The key, researchers stress, is consistency rather than intensity. Even 15 minutes of daily practice using apps, podcasts, or conversation groups can yield measurable cognitive improvements. The Lifestyle Desk has compiled a guide to the most effective language-learning tools, including digital platforms that use spaced repetition to reinforce vocabulary retention. For those who prefer immersive experiences, local conversation groups and cultural associations offer low-pressure environments to practise without fear of judgment. ## Looking Ahead The implications of this research extend beyond individual health. As global populations age, the potential for multilingualism to reduce the burden on healthcare systems is significant. Policymakers in the UK, where only 38% of adults speak a second language (compared to 56% in the EU), are beginning to take notice. The Department for Education recently announced a pilot programme offering free language courses to adults over 50, citing the cognitive benefits as a key driver. The message from the scientific community is clear: learning another language is not just about ordering a croissant with confidence—it is about building a brain that can withstand the test of time. For monoglots everywhere, the excuse to start has never been better.
